title: Chocolate fudge
subtitle: Proper cooked fudge beaten to a smooth, firm set
excerpt: Cream, sugar, butter, and cocoa cooked to soft-ball stage, then beaten as it cools to develop the fine grain that makes fudge fudge rather than toffee. A sugar thermometer is essential.
sourceNotes: Cooked fudge (distinguished from the condensed-milk shortcut) is documented in British confectionery manuals from the late nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, covers sugar-boiled confections in the confectionery chapter. Proper fudge is cooked to soft-ball stage (112-116°C) and beaten during cooling to form fine sugar crystals: the technique that distinguishes it from toffee.

content.text: and beaten vigorously as it cools. The beating breaks the large sugar crystals that form during cooking into tiny ones, giving fudge its characteristic smooth, slightly crumbly texture. Condensed milk versions skip the thermometer but also skip the crystal structure. This is the real thing.

Sugar syrup at 116°C causes serious burns. Use a heavy-based pan, keep children away during cooking, and hold the pan handle when stirring. If hot syrup contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Put the {{caster-sugar}}, {{double-cream}}, {{whole-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, and {{golden-syrup}} in a medium heavy-based saucepan. Stir over a low heat until the sugar dissolves and the mixture is smooth. Bring to a boil over medium heat, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking. Cook to 112-116°C (soft-ball stage). This takes 15-20 minutes. Do not rush it: too high a heat causes the milk to catch on the base.

Remove the pan from the heat and stir in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Leave the fudge in the pan, undisturbed, until it cools to around 60°C (warm to the touch but not scalding, about 15-20 minutes). Then beat vigorously with a wooden spoon for 5-10 minutes until the mixture thickens, loses its gloss, and becomes grainy at the edges. This is correct: the grain is forming. Pour immediately into the lined tin and smooth the top. Leave to set at room temperature for at least 2 hours before cutting into squares.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 600 g (36 pieces, from a 20 cm square tin)
